focus: correctness: concurrency races, atomicity, crash-safety (temp+fsync+rename, lock heartbeat/single-flight, partial writes)

title: Lock refresh can overwrite a newly reclaimed holder
detail: refreshLoopLock() reads the current holder, checks only ownerPid, then blindly rewrites the lock with writeLoopLockAtomic(). A stale reclaimer can concurrently move the old lock aside and create a new exclusive lock. If the old owner read before reclaim and refreshes after the new owner acquired, its rename overwrites the new lock. The CLI refresh path passes only ownerPid, so there is no acquisition nonce or generation to make refresh conditional.
claim: A delayed heartbeat from a stale-but-still-running owner can resurrect that owner over a newer claimant, allowing two loop hosts to believe they own the same packet lock.
counterevidenceSought: Checked the acquire/reclaim path and CLI adapter for a lock identity token beyond PID; none is persisted or passed through refresh. Host-local single-flight is optional and the CLI acquire path calls acquireLoopLock() without it.
alternativeExplanation: The workflow may normally kill or abandon a stale owner before another process reclaims the lock.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade only if workflow evidence proves a process whose heartbeat exceeded the stale threshold cannot ever resume and call refreshLoopLock().
suggestedFix: Add an acquisition nonce/generation to LoopLockData, return it to the holder, and require refresh/release to prove the same lock identity under a compare-and-swap style critical section; alternatively serialize refresh and reclaim through the same writer lock.

title: Diff-gated JSONL append can lose concurrent rows
detail: appendJsonlIfChangedAtomic() reads the entire target file, builds a new whole-file payload, then replaces the file with writeTextAtomic(). There is no lock or reread inside a critical section. Two processes appending different rows can both read the same prefix, pass the fingerprint checks, and then the last rename wins, dropping the other row. The helper is used by command subprocesses for the status ledger.
claim: The append helper is atomic as a replace, not atomic as an append; concurrent writers can silently lose a valid JSONL row.
counterevidenceSought: Checked the unit coverage around atomic-state.vitest.ts:155; it verifies duplicate suppression and changed-row appends in one process, but not concurrent writers.
alternativeExplanation: Current workflow callers may be serialized by an outer loop lock, making the helper effectively single-writer in deployed paths.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if every current and intended caller is proven to hold a mutually exclusive lock for the target ledger before calling this helper, and the helper is documented as single-writer only.
suggestedFix: Put the read-last-fingerprint and append under the shared writer lock, or switch to true append semantics with O_APPEND plus a locked duplicate check. Add a two-process test where both writers append distinct rows to the same ledger.

title: JSONL merge writer omits fsync before rename
detail: writeJsonlRecordsAtomic() writes a temp file and renames it, but it never fsyncs the temp file or parent directory. The shared text writer performs temp write, temp fsync, rename, and parent-directory fsync. A host or power crash after the merge returns can still lose the supposedly committed merged state log.
claim: The JSONL merge path is atomic against torn readers, but it does not provide the crash durability implied by the runtime's temp+fsync+rename state-safety contract.
counterevidenceSought: Looked for an fsync in writeJsonlRecordsAtomic() and in its caller mergeJsonlUnderLock(); the lock protects concurrency but does not add durability.
alternativeExplanation: The intended guarantee may be process-crash safety only, not host/power-loss durability.
downgradeTrigger: Drop if the JSONL merge contract explicitly excludes fsync durability and only promises readers never see partial files.
suggestedFix: Reuse writeTextAtomic() for the JSONL merge payload, or copy its temp fsync plus parent-directory fsync sequence into writeJsonlRecordsAtomic().
